steps.title: Block the spread (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a wide, low rectangle for the overall footprint of the open book. Tilt it slightly — books on a counter rarely sit perfectly square to the viewer.

steps.title: Mark the spine (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, draw a short vertical line at the middle of the rectangle — this is the valley where the two pages meet. Notice the angle of your rectangle; the spine line should tilt to match.

steps.title: Curve the pages (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, redraw the top and bottom edges of the rectangle as shallow curves that dip down toward the spine in the middle. Open pages aren't flat — they bow up at the outer edges.

steps.title: Add the page thickness (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, draw a second line just below each outer edge to suggest the stack of pages. A small thickness is all you need.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the silhouette
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace the outer outline of the book in confident passes — the curved top and bottom, the two outer edges, and the page thickness lines. Land each line cleanly where it meets the next.

steps.title: Ink the spine valley (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, draw the spine line down the middle as a slightly curved stroke, deeper in the center where the book is opened most. This single line makes the spread read as two pages, not one flat surface.

steps.title: Suggest the text (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, add three or four short horizontal lines on each page to suggest lines of text. Keep them light and don't fill the page — just enough to hint that it's a book, not a blank pad.
